meta.title: Ends Sunday
meta.theme: how deadlines manufacture desire in retail

format.format_verified: 2026-07: CAT 2025 used 2 four-sentence TITA jumbles per slot; re-verify against each new official paper

meta.learning_objective: trace a reference like the same lever back to the exact sentence that set it up

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. Scarcity and urgency in retail psychology are standard behavioural economics; no specific text adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Para Jumbles milestone); PARA_JUMBLES_BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: Easy-tier closer: a reference chain (the discovery, the same lever, none of this) that must be walked in order. First taste of the false pair. Owner review pending.

sentences.label: A
sentences.text: Online stores push the same lever harder, with countdown clocks and warnings that only two seats remain at this price.

sentences.label: B
sentences.text: None of this changes what the product is worth; it changes how the decision feels, and by the time the feeling fades, the receipt is already printed.

sentences.label: C
sentences.text: A deadline converts a mild interest into a small emergency, which is why the sign says the offer ends on Sunday rather than that it ends eventually.

sentences.label: D
sentences.text: Retail sales are engineered around a simple discovery about human beings: we want things more when we might lose the chance to have them.

links.explanation: D states the discovery: fear of losing a chance sharpens desire. C shows the tool built on it. A deadline is exactly a chance that will be lost. The principle hands off to its instrument.
A says online stores push "the same lever" harder. A lever has to exist before it can be the same one. C built it: the deadline. This reference settles A's position almost by itself.
B opens with "None of this". The "this" gathers the whole toolkit, signs and clocks and warnings together, and delivers the verdict on all of it. A wrap-up that wide comes last.

explanation.macro_pattern_note: One discovery about people, then the tool built on it, then the tool sharpened, then the verdict on the whole machine. The paragraph is a chain of references: the discovery, the same lever, none of this. Each link picks up the one before it.

movement.label: D
movement.why_here: D is the foundation: a general truth about human wanting. It mentions nothing that needs a prior sentence, and every other sentence spends what it earns.

movement.label: C
movement.why_here: C turns the truth into a mechanism. The deadline is the discovery weaponised, and the Sunday sign makes it concrete.

movement.label: A
movement.why_here: A escalates. "The same lever" openly points back at C's deadline, and the countdown clock is the deadline with the volume turned up.

movement.label: B
movement.why_here: B steps back and judges everything at once. "None of this" needs a full toolkit to point at, so B can only close.

tempting_orders.why_tempting: D talks about retail sales and A talks about online stores. Shops with shops feels like a pair, so A wants to sit right after D.
tempting_orders.where_it_breaks: A leans on "the same lever", and after D alone there is no lever, only a discovery. The lever is C's deadline. Lock D and A together and A's central reference points at nothing. A pair that feels natural is still only a guess until the whole chain holds.

explanation.trap_named: The pull was to pair the two shop sentences because they share a topic. Topics do not order sentences; references do. Before you lock any pair, find the exact word that ties them, and check that nothing else is needed in between.
explanation.solving_habit: When a sentence says "the same X", stop and find the sentence that built X. It must come somewhere before, and usually right before.

mentor.challenge: This paragraph is a chain with three links. Each sentence picks up something the one before it left. Find the chain.
mentor.paragraph_plain: The author explains a trick every shopper has felt. Sales are built on one discovery: we want things more when the chance to get them might disappear. The deadline is that discovery turned into a tool. A sign saying the offer ends Sunday turns mild interest into a small emergency. Online shops crank the same tool harder, with countdown clocks and warnings that only two seats are left. And here is the verdict: none of it changes what the product is worth. It changes how deciding feels. By the time the feeling passes, you have already paid.
mentor.takeaway: Topics group sentences. References order them.
